meta.title: Claude Builder — 21 Days of AI Building

days.title: Problem Framing
days.idea:
Most people come to Claude with a fuzzy ask and blame the answer. The skill this week is problem framing. Naming who has the problem, what it actually is, and what done looks like before you touch a tool.

Today you do two things. You fill in the first two sections of the Build Brief in plain English. Then with that clarity in hand, you install Claude Desktop and Claude Code, and make one file appear on your screen using only conversation.

Skill clarity. Tool clarity. Both today.
days.howToClaude: I am going to describe a problem in plain language. Help me sharpen it. Who has it. What it actually is. No solution language. Then help me narrate the outcome in five to seven sentences as if it already exists. Walk through what the person does, what they see, what they get.
stepByStep: Open Claude.ai (claude.ai). Paste the prompt above.
Describe the problem you are circling. Let Claude push back until the statement is in plain human language.
Narrate the outcome in five to seven sentences. Save sections 1 and 2 of your Build Brief.
Install Claude Desktop from claude.ai/download.
Install Claude Code following the prompts. Open it. Type: "Make a file called hello.html that says 'Hi, I am [your name] and I am building this week.' Open it in my browser when done."
days.deploy: Two things in the cohort space: a screenshot of the hello-you file, and your problem statement in two lines.
days.reflection: What got clearer in your problem statement that you had been hiding from yourself?

days.title: Solution Shape
days.idea:
Every solution has a shape. Information, workflow, data persistence, or autonomous actions. The skill today is recognizing which shape your problem actually wants, not which one excites you.

Most people pick the wrong shape because they pick the most interesting one. Claude is the tool that helps you stress-test the choice.

By end of day you will know what you are building, what form it takes, and which Claude surface you will use to build it.
days.howToClaude: Here is my problem and outcome from yesterday: [paste]. Walk me through the four shapes: information, workflow, data persistence, autonomous actions. For each, tell me the test from the Build Brief and ask me to answer honestly. At the end, recommend the shape and explain your reasoning.
stepByStep: Open Claude.ai (claude.ai). Paste sections 1 and 2 of your Build Brief plus the prompt above.
Answer Claude's questions honestly. Especially the test questions for each shape.
Pick your shape. If two feel right, pick the simpler one.
Write section 4: "My solution is a [shape] that [does what] for [who]."
Write section 5: where you will build. Claude.ai, Project, Cowork, or Claude Code. Start at Claude.ai. Move only if you need to.
days.deploy: Post your one-line form sentence in the cohort. "My solution is a [shape] that [does what] for [who]."
days.reflection: Did you almost pick a more exciting shape that did not match your problem? What pulled you there?

days.title: The Quick Win
days.idea:
A quick win is not a full solution. It is the smallest piece that proves the shape works. The skill today is restraint, building exactly what is needed to test the shape and nothing more.

The tool is whichever Claude surface you chose yesterday. The iteration log is the small habit that turns building into a thinking practice.

By end of day you have a working artifact and the first row of your log.
days.howToClaude: Here is my Build Brief so far. Here is my quick win: [one sentence]. Build only that. Single file. Plain. No extras. When done, suggest one small thing I could change tomorrow that would teach me something.
stepByStep: Write section 6 of your Build Brief: one sentence describing the quick win, doable in two hours.
Open the surface you chose yesterday. Paste your brief plus the prompt above.
Let Claude build. Test the result. Use it once.
Open section 7 of your brief, the iteration log. Add row one with today's date, what you built, why, and "yes, it works."
Save everything. Foundation is done.
days.deploy: Post the quick win in the cohort. A screenshot, a URL, or a paragraph if your shape was information.
days.reflection: What did you almost add that you held back on? The Brief works when you can say no to extras.

days.title: Public Voice
days.idea:
Now you turn your project outward. The skill is clear public voice: saying what you do and who it is for in language a stranger can act on.

Most landing pages fail because the writer is performing instead of communicating. The tool is Claude.ai, used as a draft partner that pushes you toward shorter, plainer, more honest sentences.

Today is words. Tomorrow you build the page.
days.howToClaude: Here is my Build Brief. I am writing the copy for a landing page. Draft a hero headline (one line), a subhead (one sentence), and one call to action. Make it sound like a real person, not a marketing site. Then ask me three questions that would make it sharper.
stepByStep: Open Claude.ai (claude.ai). Paste your Build Brief plus the prompt above.
Read Claude's draft out loud. Does it sound like you, or like a template?
Answer the three questions. Let Claude rewrite.
Iterate two or three more rounds until the headline says exactly what you do.
Save the copy in a doc or in your project Notion.
days.deploy: Paste your hero headline and CTA in the cohort. One line each.
days.reflection: What word did Claude suggest that you kept? What word did you replace because it was not yours?

days.title: The Landing Page
days.idea:
Today the copy meets the page. The skill is translating words into a layout that respects them. The tool is Claude Code, where you describe the page in plain English and watch structure appear.

The trick is to lead with feeling, not with features. Tell Claude what the page should feel like before you tell it what should be on it.
days.howToClaude: Build me a single-page landing page. Here is the copy: [paste]. I want it to feel [warm, bold, quiet, playful, premium, friendly]. Single HTML file with inline CSS. Mobile first. Clean typography. One section per content block. Keep it simple.
stepByStep: Open Claude Code in a fresh folder for your project.
Paste your copy plus the prompt above with your feeling words.
Let Claude Code build the file. Open it in your browser.
Walk away for ten minutes. Come back fresh. List three things that work and three that are off.
Tell Claude exactly what is off and how you want it instead. Iterate two or three rounds.
days.deploy: Save v1 of the page. Take a screenshot. Compare against the feeling words you started with.
days.reflection: What did you ask for that you could not have specified to a designer six months ago?

days.title: One Action
days.idea:
A page that does nothing is a poster. A page that does one thing is a tool. The skill today is restraint again: choosing the single action you want a visitor to take and refusing all the others.

The tool is Claude Code, wiring one call to action that actually works end to end. Email capture, calendar link, contact form, a button that does something real. One. Not three.
days.howToClaude: Add a [email capture form using Buttondown, calendar booking link, contact form] to my landing page. When someone uses it, they should [describe what happens]. Keep everything else the same.
stepByStep: Decide the one action before opening Claude Code. Write it down in plain words.
Set up the service that powers the action. Buttondown for email. Cal.com (cal.com) for calendar. Use the free tier.
Copy the embed code or URL from the service.
Open Claude Code with yesterday's project. Paste the prompt above plus any code from the service.
Test the CTA yourself. Submit a real entry. Confirm it lands where it should.
days.deploy: Save the file. Use the CTA one more time as a stranger would.
days.reflection: Did you almost add a second CTA? Why? Notice that pull. It is the pull that kills clarity.
